text:
21 JULY 1959

I. THE COMMUNIST BLOC
Khrushchev cancels his Scandinavian
tour.
Gromyko rejects Hammarskjold's ef-
forts to persuade USSR to moderate
its propaganda attacks on Iran.

II. ASIA-AFRICA
South Korean Army chief who has
led anticorruption drive may soon
be replaced.
India's foreign-exchange reserves
continue to decline.
Singapore's left-wing government
moving effectively to consolidate
its position.

LATE ITEM
Iraq--Army moves to impose tighter
controls on Communist dominated
Popular Resistance Force

text:
Moscow Again Refuses to Stop Propaganda Attacks on Iran

Soviet Foreign Minister Gromyko, in rejecting UN Sec-
retary General Hammarskjold's efforts at Geneva to obtain a
Soviet agreement to moderate radio propaganda attacks on
Iran, indicated to Hammarskjold that Soviet leaders are fully
convinced that the US-Iranian defense pact will inevitably lead
to the establishment of US bases in Iran. Gromyko declared
that direct talks with the Iranians would be of no use unless
Tehran first gave "assurances" that it would be more "flexi-
ble" regarding Iranian defense plans and American military
aid.

The secretary general's approach to Gromyko follows a
plan worked out in mid-June with Iranian Foreign Minister
Abdoh before he left New York. (TOP SECRET DAUNT) Be-
fore Hammarskjold met with Gromyko, he sent him detailed
Iranian complaints against Soviet propaganda, including in-
formation on the location in the southern USSR of the clandes-
tine "National Voice of Iran." The letter indicated that Iran
desired to normalize relations with the USSR and was pre-
pared to do so once the propaganda broadcasts ceased, and
it contained the secretary general's offer to act as intermediary.
Moscow's new rebuff suggests that Soviet leaders still believe
their propaganda can compel Tehran to take the "first step"
to repair relations. The Shah, however, continues to main-
tain a policy of giving no concessions, despite some domestic
pressures to appease the USSR.

Meanwhile, after less than two weeks in office, Foreign
Minister Abdoh has resigned--on grounds of health--rather
than become a scapegoat for a possible failure of Iran's policy
toward the USSR. Prime Minister Eqbal has asked the Iranian ambassador to
Japan, Abbas Aram, to accept the post of foreign minister if
his "health is favorable
